这起罪行的受害者都是哑巴，甚至在他们还没有被变成汤之前就无助地为自己的理由辩护。但是尼希米亚·卡尔霍恩船长船上的绿海龟有一个冠军亨利·伯格，他是一个决心为它们的同类挺身而出的人。1866年5月的一个早晨，伯格主动登上了这艘从佛罗里达来的纵帆船，船上载着一批海龟前往曼哈顿的富尔顿鱼类市场(Fulton Fish Market)。在甲板下，他发现一百多只大海龟像活生生的行李一样堆放在一起。在它们向北航行的几个星期里，这些动物被剥夺了食物和水。更糟糕的是，船长把它们翻了个底朝天，让它们动弹不得，并用刺穿它们鳍状肢的绳子把它们绑在一起，造成的伤口在海上航行数周后仍在渗出。在海龟穿孔的四肢和晶莹剔透的眼睛里，伯格不仅目睹了数周的身体痛苦和剥夺，还见证了“智力上的痛苦”。他想他看到眼泪从他们的眼睛里流下来，在下面的甲板上留下斑驳的斑点。
就在一个月前，伯格在纽约成立了美国防止虐待动物协会，这是一个致力于结束人类“对…的严重无知、粗心大意、冷漠和肆意残忍”的组织。野蛮的创造。“。伯格将在接下来的20年里发现，将动物权利从人权中解脱出来是多么困难。
亨利·伯格的动机不是对动物的不同寻常的热爱，而是对人类残忍的憎恨。他五十三岁，最近才开始从事这项事业。作为工业财富的继承人，他职业生涯的大部分时间都花在了对文学名气的徒劳追求上。在美国内战期间担任俄罗斯外交官的短暂时间里，伯格目睹了圣彼得堡卡车司机对马的残酷对待，这在他身上激起了一种类似于皈依的经历。在拜访了成立于1824年的英国皇家防止虐待动物协会的领导人后，他回到美国，决心建立一个类似的组织。
州立法机构很快授予伯格他的新成立的美国防止虐待动物协会(ASPCA)的章程，然后通过了他起草的一项法律，规定“恶意杀害、致残、伤害、酷刑或残忍地殴打任何马、骡子、牛、牛、羊或其他动物都是犯罪行为。”
在1866年之前，已经有20个州和地区通过了反暴法，重点是保护牲畜。由于这些法律很少得到执行，伯格精心设计了一个更强有力的执行机制。该法令授权他的ASPCA任命自己的“代理人”，即佩戴徽章的官员，他们被授权干预虐待动物的行为，呼吁警察执法，甚至自己逮捕他们。事实证明，这是该法律最重要的创新之一，授权伯格不仅恳求更好的行为，而且要求更好的行为。这条法律很有说服力。正如历史学家苏珊·皮尔森(Susan Pearson)所说，该法规“使犯罪行为更明确地包括对动物本身的暴力行为，而不是侵犯财产权或扰乱公共秩序。”正如伯格解释自己的法律一样，这包括保护海龟免受不必要的痛苦的权利。
因此，伯格认为卡尔霍恩船长是个十足的罪犯，因为他把海龟运到一个“不自然”的位置，看起来显然很残忍。但卡尔霍恩坚称自己是无辜的，并解释说，这是这一行业的标准做法，也是最不有害的运输方式。它们背部坚硬的甲壳保护它们免受伤害，否则当它们在船舱里为争取自由而挥舞时，它们柔软的腹部就会受到伤害。
伯格并不买账。根据新的反垄断法，他行使了自己的权力，命令两名警察逮捕船长和他的全体船员。贵族伯格-可能戴着他惯常的大礼帽和手杖-把心怀不满的渔民带到了被称为古墓的市法院和监狱。在那里，他们将面临一种奇怪的新正义，伯格认为，这项法律肯定赋予了即使是前往汤锅的爬行动物一定程度的权利。
长期以来，海龟一直是水手的主要鲜肉来源，但在19世纪，它不幸地取悦了欧洲和美国的富人，他们发现海龟壳下的胶状“绿色脂肪”是一种特别的美味。在各种可食用的物种中，美食家特别渴望绿海龟，这是一种海草饲料，通常长到四五百磅，有时甚至是两倍。从尼加拉瓜到卡罗莱纳，猎人们每年使用渔网和长矛捕获数以千计的这种迁徙动物。或者，当雌性海龟离开安全的大海，在月光照耀的海滩上产卵时，猎人会将它们倾覆，然后要么从容地屠宰它们，要么把它们拖到围栏里，然后把它们送到北方市场的长途航行中。
纽约的富人们通过加入“海龟俱乐部”来表达他们对海龟肉的热情，他们每个季节都会聚集在一起用餐，每道菜都以海龟的肉为中心-汤、牛排、杂烩和沙拉。纽约的一家餐厅每年烹调超过25000磅的海龟肉，并将汤和牛排运往遥远的伦敦。由于龟菜已经成为“美食界最大的奢侈品”，正如人们所观察到的那样，任何动物的朋友都不会希望扰乱这一贸易。“必须也将会有海龟。”
当伯格逮捕卡尔霍恩船长时，他从未想过要干扰海龟渔业。他喜欢告诉观众，他喜欢乌龟，但也喜欢乌龟汤。在他看来，以人道的方式夺走动物的生命并不是残忍的行为。“否则屠夫就会受到这项指控，”他推论道，“所有吃肉的人在某种程度上都是帮凶。”正如他在他的职业生涯中无数次解释的那样，反暴法从未质疑过人们使用动物的权利，只质疑他们虐待动物的权利。
但是，一个人能虐待一只没有感情的动物吗？在卡尔霍恩船长的审判中，辩方产生了一位专家证人，霍华德·格恩西(Howard Guernsey)博士，他作证说，海龟的“神经组织”是“非常低级的”，使它们无法忍受痛苦。在他看来，刺穿生物的鳍状肢并用绳子绑住它们，并不会比人类被蚊子叮咬的感觉更深刻。法庭记录中没有解释根西岛有权就海龟的生理学发表讲话，但在他的解释中可能会发现他的可靠性的某种程度，他解释说“死亡并不像牙痛那么痛苦。”“死亡没有牙痛那么痛苦”，这一点在法庭记录中没有得到解释，但他的解释可能会发现他的可靠性。他声称“从未见过任何人在痛苦中死去”。由此，他推断，如果伯格的说法是正确的，即海龟仰卧死亡的速度更快，那么这些可怜的生物可能会认为这是一种幸事。
动物死后会遭受痛苦吗？乌龟有道德和法律地位吗？这些事情已经够模糊的了，但是霍根法官法庭上的辩论因为一些基本的生物学概念的混乱而更加混乱。正如纽约的一篇论文总结了审判中的“棘手问题”一样，辩方专家格恩西博士将它们定位在甲壳类和两栖类之间的中间地带，“就像螃蟹、龙虾或牡蛎”。
在这个反垄断法的早期测试案例中，霍根法官的怀疑是显而易见的。为了在与伯格的比赛中得分，法官问他，把海龟从水中赶走是否首先不是一种残忍的行为。他暗示，被推向合乎逻辑的结论是，一项防止残酷对待海龟的法律可能会剥夺人类享受海龟肉体的乐趣。伯格表现出比当天法庭上的大多数人都更好地掌握生物学，他解释说，海龟没有因为被从海洋中移走而受到伤害，因为它们生活在陆地上。
伯格嘲笑辩方的说法，即海龟不是动物，而是鱼。一个世纪以来，新的比较解剖学的专家们一直在提出相互竞争的动物分类方案。但他们中没有人怀疑海龟属于爬行动物家族，或者爬行动物是动物。像他那个时代的许多富有的年轻人一样，伯格从来没有费心读完大学，在平淡无奇的两年后离开了哥伦比亚大学。但在那天的法庭上，他扮演了一个被激怒的专家的角色。他讲授说，自然界分为三个“王国”--动物、植物和矿物。他问道，如果海龟不是“动物”，那么剩下的选择中会有哪些呢？
除了倡导他们自私的动物学，卡尔霍恩船长的律师们提出了一个更可信的论点，认为新的反对虐待动物的州法律从来没有旨在将海龟等“低级”物种包括在内。这是一个合理的法律问题，并很快引发了一场关于人类对其他物种的道德义务的激烈的公开辩论。新的反垄断法提出了一项激进的主张，即动物有权受到保护，免受本可避免的痛苦和苦难。但是，即使人类原则上接受了这一观点，这一责任是否也适用于所有生物呢？这种针对残忍的保护到底起到了多大的作用呢？伯格一直往下想。当“伟大的造物主”给了“穷人鄙视乌龟”生命时，他也给了它“感觉和一定的权利，也给了我们自己”。
其他人则嚎叫抗议。如果伯格和他在ASPCA的追随者们的目标是捍卫“所有动物的生命”，那么这种滑坡将导致他们对蟾蜍和鳄鱼、蛤蜊和贻贝-甚至是有害的蚊子-的福祉感到荒谬的担忧。伯格在一系列的信件和演讲中进行了反击，谴责他的敌人是“野蛮和不思进取的人”。对他来说，逮捕卡尔霍恩上尉是对他为捍卫所有创造物而提出的更广泛的道德主张的一个明显而有争议的考验。
但伯格逮捕卡尔霍恩上尉的动机，与其说是出于他对所有生物的宇宙共鸣，不如说是因为他认识到，反腐败运动需要宣传，报业的朋友和敌人都将无能为力地抵制这样一个奇怪的案件。就像之前和之后的许多其他道德斗士一样，伯格得出的结论是，激起媒体的嘲笑比在媒体的冷漠下萎靡不振要好得多。事实上，他并不是偶然发现卡尔霍恩船长的一船海龟，而是在当地一家报纸上读到海龟到来的消息后才找到它们的。那天，他来到富尔顿鱼市，寻找一种“一流的轰动”。
啊，真灵。这起案件引发了公众在每一家城市报纸、全国各地的专栏以及街头对“著名乌龟案”的讨论。几年后，伯格写道，这就是“我渴望的刺激”。“此后不到一周，就有一百万人阅读了这本书--并且第一次想到了这件事！”
伯格几乎没有这样的想法。报纸嘲笑“多愁善感的伯格”代表卑微生物的“可悲的诉求”。曼哈顿的一家酒馆在门前放了一只大活海龟，垫在玉米皮床上，头下放了一个枕头。在他上方，店主张贴了这条消息：
我们不想伤害“防止虐待动物协会”的任何成员或其主席亨利·伯格的感情，所以在这只可怜的海龟生命的最后几天里，我们已经尽了我们所能的去安慰它。然而，他被任命为死刑，周四和周五将被端上汤和牛排。邀请上述协会的成员和其他人前来，为他的记忆伸张正义。
几天后，法院宣布卡尔霍恩船长无罪。伯格唯一的安慰来自于法官驳回了船长以“恶意起诉和非法监禁”为由对他提起的反诉。伯格对这一裁决表示欢迎，认为这证明了ASPCA特工有权追查这些案件，而不必担心自己会被监禁。不知不觉中，当州立法机构通过这项反暴力法时，它向伯格和他的副手发放了一张许可证，可以激起一场关于人类对其他动物权力的限制的公开讨论。
摘自“他的种族的叛徒：亨利·伯格和动物权利运动的诞生”。版权所有©2020 Ernest Freeberg。可从阿歇特图书集团(Hachette Book Group，Inc.)旗下的基本图书公司(Basic Books)获得。
欧内斯特·弗里伯格(Ernest Freeberg)是田纳西大学(University Of Tennessee)杰出的人文科学教授和历史系主任。他是“他的种族的叛徒：亨利·伯格和动物权利运动的诞生”和“爱迪生时代：电灯和现代美国的发明”等书籍的作者。
note_en:
The victims of the crime were mute, helpless to plead their cause even before they had been turned into soup. But the green turtles in the hold of Captain Nehemiah Calhoun’s boat had a champion in Henry Bergh, one human determined to stand up for their kind. On a May morning in 1866, Bergh boarded the schooner  Active, just arrived from Florida with a shipment of turtles for Manhattan’s Fulton Fish Market. Belowdecks, he found over a hundred large turtles stacked like living luggage. For weeks during their voyage north, the animals had been deprived of food and water. Worse still, the captain had flipped them upside down to immobilize them and had bound them together with a rope pierced through their flippers, creating wounds that still oozed after weeks at sea. In the turtles’ punctured limbs and glassy eyes, Bergh witnessed not only weeks of physical pain and deprivation but also “intellectual suffering.” He thought he saw tears falling from their eyes, dappling the deck below.
 Only a month before, Bergh had founded the American Society for the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals in New York, an organization dedicated to ending humanity’s “gross ignorance, thoughtlessness, indifference, and wanton cruelty to…brute creation.” Bergh would spend the next two decades discovering just how hard it was to untangle animal rights from human privileges.
  Henry Bergh was motivated not by an unusual love of animals but by a hatred of human cruelty. Fifty-three years old, he had come to this cause only recently. Heir to an industrial fortune, he had spent most of his career in a fruitless quest for literary fame. During a brief stint as a diplomat to Russia during the U.S. Civil War, Bergh had witnessed brutal treatment of horses by St. Petersburg teamsters that provoked in him something like a conversion experience. After visiting the leaders of England’s Royal Society for the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals, founded in 1824, he returned to the United States determined to establish a similar organization.
 The state legislature soon granted Bergh a charter for his new American Society for the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals (ASPCA), then passed a law he had drafted, making it a crime to “maliciously kill, maim, wound, torture, or cruelly beat any horse, mule, ox, cattle, sheep, or other animal.”
 Before 1866 twenty states and territories had passed anticruelty laws, focusing on the protection of livestock. Because these laws were rarely enforced, Bergh crafted a much stronger enforcement mechanism. The statute empowered his ASPCA to appoint its own “agents,” badge-wearing officers who were authorized to intervene in acts of animal cruelty, to call on police to enforce the law, and even to make their own arrests. This proved to be one of the law’s most important innovations, empowering Bergh not only to plead for better behavior but also to demand it. This law had teeth. As historian Susan Pearson puts it, the statute “made the crime consist more clearly of violence against animals themselves rather than the violation of property rights or disruption of the public order.” As Bergh interpreted his own law, that included the right of turtles to be protected from unnecessary pain.
 Thus, Bergh considered Captain Calhoun a rank criminal for shipping his turtles in an “unnatural” position that seemed obviously cruel. But Calhoun protested his innocence, explaining that this was standard practice in the trade and the least harmful way to transport them. Their backs’ hard carapace protected them from the damage they would otherwise inflict on their softer bellies as they flailed for their freedom in the hold of a ship.
 Bergh was not buying it. Exercising his authority under the new anticruelty law, he ordered two policemen to arrest the captain and his entire crew. The patrician Bergh—likely sporting his usual top hat and cane—led the disgruntled fishermen to the city court and jail known as the Tombs. There they would face a strange new kind of justice, a law that Bergh felt sure had granted even reptiles bound for the soup pot some measure of rights.
  Long a staple source of fresh meat for sailors, turtles had the misfortune in the nineteenth century of pleasing the palates of wealthy Europeans and Americans, who found the gelatinous “green fat” under the turtle’s shell a particular delicacy. Among the various edible species, gourmands particularly hungered after the green turtle, a seagrass feeder that commonly grew to four or five hundred pounds, and sometimes twice that much. From Nicaragua to the Carolinas, hunters used nets and spears to capture thousands of these migratory animals each year. Or, when female turtles left the safety of the sea to lay their eggs on a moonlit beach, hunters capsized them, then either butchered them at their leisure or hauled them to holding pens before sending them on the long voyage to northern markets.
 Wealthy men in New York expressed their passion for turtle meat by joining “turtle clubs,” convening each season for meals in which the reptile’s flesh formed the center of every course—soups, steaks, chowders, and salads. A single restaurant in New York cooked over 25,000 pounds of turtle meat each year and shipped its soup and steaks as far away as London. Since turtle dishes had become “the greatest luxury of the epicure,” as one observed, no friend of the animals could hope to disrupt the trade. “Turtles must and will be had.”
 When Bergh arrested Captain Calhoun, he never meant to interfere with the turtle fishery. He liked to tell audiences that he was fond of turtles, but also turtle soup. Done humanely, taking an animal’s life was not an act of cruelty, as he saw it. “Otherwise the butcher exposes himself to this charge,” he reasoned, “and all who eat flesh are to a certain extent accomplices.” As he would explain countless times over the course of his career, the anticruelty law never questioned people’s right to use animals, only their right to abuse them.
 But can one abuse an animal that has no feelings? At Captain Calhoun’s trial, the defense produced an expert witness, a Dr. Howard Guernsey, who testified that the “nervous organization” of turtles was “of the very lowest order,” making them incapable of suffering. In his view, piercing the creatures’ flippers and binding them with cord made no more impression than a human would feel from a mosquito bite. The source of Guernsey’s authority to speak on the physiology of turtles was not explained in the court records, but some measure of his reliability might be found in his explanation that “dying is not as painful as toothache.” He claimed to have “never seen any person dying in agony.” From this he reasoned that, if Bergh was correct in his claim that turtles died more quickly when placed on their backs, the poor creatures might count this a blessing.
 Do animals suffer when they die? Do turtles have moral and legal standing? These matters were murky enough, but the debate in Justice Hogan’s courtroom was further muddled by confusion about some basic biological concepts. As one New York paper summarized the “knotty question” at stake in the trial,   The defense’s expert, Dr. Guernsey, located them in a middle ground between crustaceans and amphibians, “like a crab, lobster, or oyster.”
  In this early test case of the anticruelty law, Judge Hogan’s skepticism was clear. Seeking to score a point against Bergh, the judge asked him if removing turtles from the water in the first place was not an act of cruelty. Pushed to its logical conclusion, he implied, a law preventing cruelty to turtles might deprive humankind of the pleasures of their flesh. Displaying a better grasp of biology than most in the courtroom that day, Bergh explained that sea turtles were not harmed by being removed from the sea because they lived a portion of their lives on land.
 Bergh ridiculed the defense’s claim that turtles were not animals but fishes. For a century, experts in the new science of comparative anatomy had been proposing competing schemes for classifying animals. But none of them doubted that turtles belonged in the reptile family or that reptiles were animals. Like many wealthy young men in his day, Bergh had never bothered to finish college, leaving Columbia after two undistinguished years. But in court that day, he assumed the role of an exasperated expert. All of nature divides into three “kingdoms,” he lectured—animal, vegetable, mineral. If turtles are not “animal,” he asked, then which of the remaining choices might they be?
 In addition to advocating their self-serving zoology, Captain Calhoun’s lawyers made a more plausible argument, suggesting that the new state law against cruelty to animals never aimed to include “lower” species such as turtles. Here was a valid legal question, and one that soon provoked a boisterous public debate about humanity’s moral obligation to other species. The new anticruelty law staked a radical claim that animals enjoy the right to be protected from avoidable pain and suffering. But even if humans accepted this idea in principle, did that duty extend to all creatures? Just how far down the chain of being did this protection against cruelty go? Bergh thought all the way down. When the “Great Creator” gave life to “the poor despised turtle,” He also gave it “feeling and certain rights as well as ourselves.”
 Others howled in protest. If Bergh and his followers in the ASPCA aimed to defend “all animal life,” that slippery slope would lead them to an absurd concern for the well-being of toads and crocodiles, clams and mussels—even the pernicious mosquito. Bergh fought back in a series of letters and lectures, denouncing his foes as “brutal and unthinking people.” For him, the arrest of Captain Calhoun served as a visible and controversial test case for a wider moral claim he was making in defense of all Creation.
 But Bergh’s arrest of Captain Calhoun was motivated less by his cosmic empathy for all living things than by his recognition that the anticruelty movement needed publicity and that newspaper friends and foes would be helpless to resist such a curious case. Like many other moral crusaders before and since, Bergh concluded that it was better to provoke the press’s ridicule than to wither under its apathy. He had not, in fact, stumbled across Captain Calhoun’s shipload of turtles accidentally, but had sought them out after reading of their arrival in a local paper. He had come to the Fulton Fish Market that day searching for a “first-class sensation.”
 It worked. The case provoked public conversation about the “celebrated turtle case” in every city newspaper, in columns across the country, and in the streets as well. Here was “the excitement I desired,” Bergh wrote years later. “Within a week thereafter, a million of people, had read—and for the first time thought of the matter!”
 Little of that thinking went Bergh’s way. Newspapers derided the “maudlin Bergh” for his “pathetic appeals” on behalf of lowly creatures. And a Manhattan saloon placed a large live turtle in front of its premises, cushioned on a bed of cornhusks, a pillow under its head. Above him the proprietor posted this message:
 Having no desire to wound the feelings of any member of the “Society for the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals,” or of its president, Henry Bergh, Esq., we have done what we could for the comfort of the poor turtle during the few remaining days of his life. He is appointed unto death, however, and will be served in soups and steaks on Thursday and Friday. Members of the aforesaid society and others are invited to come and do justice to his memory.
 Days later the court declared Captain Calhoun innocent. Bergh’s only consolation came when the judge dismissed a countersuit that the captain had brought against him for “malicious prosecution and false imprisonment.” Bergh greeted this ruling as vindication of the right of ASPCA agents to pursue these cases without fear of being jailed themselves. Unwittingly, when the state legislature passed the anticruelty law, it issued Bergh and his deputies a license to provoke a public conversation about the limits of human power over other animals.
  Excerpted from  A Traitor to His Species: Henry Bergh and the Birth of the Animal Rights Movement . Copyright © 2020 Ernest Freeberg. Available from Basic Books, an imprint of Hachette Book Group, Inc.
  Ernest Freeberg is a distinguished professor of humanities and head of the history department at the University of Tennessee. He is the author of  A Traitor to His Species: Henry Bergh and the Birth of the Animal Rights Movement and  The Age of Edison: Electric Light and the Invention of Modern America, among other books.
title: 乌龟是动物还是鱼？
title_en: Is a Turtle an Animal or a Fish?

note:
尼泊尔加德满都-周一，昂格·丽塔·夏尔巴(Ang Rita Sherpa)在加德满都的女儿家中去世。他因在没有补充氧气的情况下攀登世界最高峰珠穆朗玛峰10次而享誉全球。他72岁。
他的家人和尼泊尔登山协会证实了他的死讯。没有具体说明原因，但他近年来一直患有多发性肺部和脑部疾病，同事们说，这些疾病可能是由于他多年来在没有瓶装氧气的情况下攀登高海拔造成的。
大多数登山者在攀登海拔8000米以上的山峰时都会使用补充氧气，登山者称这个高度为“死亡区域”，因为空气太稀薄了，以至于人体开始关闭。在他职业生涯的早期是搬运工，后来是登山导游，Ang Rita注意到他从来没有感觉到需要补充氧气，即使他为其他登山者搬运瓶装氧气也是如此。他在1983年第一次攀登珠穆朗玛峰时没有使用它，后来的九次攀登也没有使用它，最后一次是在1996年。
在他唯一次登上珠穆朗玛峰的冬季探险中，在1987-88年间，他和一名韩国登山者在恶劣的天气条件下在山顶下方迷了路，为了保暖，他们花了一整晚的时间做有氧运动。
安格丽塔保持着吉尼斯世界纪录，在没有瓶装氧气的情况下攀登珠穆朗玛峰的次数最多，这一纪录仍然是无与伦比的。(另一位夏尔巴人卡米·丽塔(Kami Rita)保持着攀登珠穆朗玛峰最多的纪录，他已经攀登了24次，但众所周知他使用的是瓶装氧气。)。
尼泊尔政府向昂丽塔颁发了几个奖项，其中最著名的是1990年的三沙克提·巴塔一等勋章(Order Of Tri Shakti Patta)。
尼泊尔总统比迪亚·德维·班达里(Bidya Devi Bhandari)在Twitter上写道，“他的死对这个国家的攀登产业来说是一个不可挽回的损失。”
昂格丽塔·夏尔巴1948年出生于尼泊尔珠穆朗玛峰地区泰晤士山附近的一个小村庄伊拉容。他的母亲乔基·夏尔巴(Chhokki Sherpa)和父亲阿亚拉·夏尔巴(Aayala Sherpa)都是农民。昂格丽塔从未接受过正规教育(直到1961年，第一位登顶珠穆朗玛峰的登山者埃德蒙·希拉里(Edmund Hillary)在昆容建造了一所学校，珠穆朗玛峰地区才建立起一所学校)。他自学了尼泊尔字母，几乎不会写自己的名字。
昂格丽塔的童年时光是在高地牧场上度过的，他放牧牦牛，种植土豆，从附近的市场运送商品。他在15岁时成为一名搬运工，并很快获得了敏捷的声誉，最终获得了雪豹的绰号。
虽然他是在珠穆朗玛峰的阴影下长大的，但他作为搬运工的第一份工作是攀登杜拉吉里(Dhaulagiri)，这是一座喜马拉雅山脉，其中包括世界第七高峰，他没有鞋子或设备。
除了10次攀登珠穆朗玛峰外，昂丽塔总共攀登了Dhaulagiri四次，以及喜马拉雅峰Cho Oyu-也是四次-以及第三高峰Kchenjuga一次。他在任何一次攀登中都没有使用补充氧气。
尼泊尔登山协会前主席Ang Tshering在接受采访时说，他曾和Ang Rita一起攀登Dhaulagiri，他发现他是那个时代最强壮的夏尔巴人。“他挑战科学和人类生理学，”他说。
1996年珠穆朗玛峰灾难后，昂格丽塔停止了攀登，那次灾难导致8人在一场猛烈的暴风雪中丧生。但他继续担任大本营经理和徒步旅行向导。
同事们说，他从来没有存过钱，也没有担心过未来。他过着幸福的生活，尽情享受退休生活。在他的妻子尼玛·卓基(Nima Chokki)几年前去世之前，他一直住在自己的家乡。然后他搬到加德满都和他的女儿多尔玛住在一起。
她留下了他，还有两个儿子，Tshewang Dorje和Furunuru，以及八个孙子。另一个儿子卡尔桑·南加尔·夏尔巴(Karsan Namgyal Sherpa)也成为了一名登山向导，他于2012年在珠穆朗玛峰探险后去世。
note_en:
KATHMANDU, Nepal — Ang Rita Sherpa, who earned global fame by climbing the world’s highest mountain, Mount Everest, 10 times without the use of supplemental oxygen, died on Monday at his daughter’s house in Kathmandu. He was 72.
 His death was confirmed by his family and by Nepal’s mountaineering associations. No cause was specified, but he had been suffering in recent years from multiple lung and brain ailments that colleagues say could have developed from his years of climbing high altitudes without bottled oxygen.
 Most climbers use supplemental oxygen when ascending peaks higher than 8,000 meters, an altitude mountaineers call the “death zone” because the air is so thin that the human body begins to shut down. Early in his career as a porter, and later as a mountain guide, Ang Rita noticed that he never felt the need for supplemental oxygen, even as he carried bottles of it for other mountaineers. He didn’t use it during his first ascent of Everest in 1983 or on his subsequent nine ascents, the last of which was in 1996.
 In his only winter expedition on Everest, in 1987-88, he and a Korean climber lost their way just below the summit in bad weather conditions and spent the whole night doing  aerobic exercises to stay warm.
 Ang Rita  holds the Guinness World Record for most climbs of Everest without bottled oxygen, a record that remains unequaled. (Another Sherpa, Kami Rita, holds the record for most total ascents of Everest, having done it 24 times, but he was known to use bottled oxygen.)
 The Nepalese government honored Ang Rita with several awards, most notably the Order of Tri Shakti Patta First Class in 1990.
 “His demise is an irreparable loss to the country’s climbing industry,” President Bidya Devi Bhandari of Nepal wrote on Twitter.
 Ang Rita Sherpa was born in 1948 in Yillajung, a tiny village near Thame in the Everest region of Nepal. His mother, Chhokki Sherpa, and his father, Aayala Sherpa, were farmers. Ang Rita never received a formal education (no school was established in the Everest region until 1961, when Edmund Hillary, the first mountaineer to reach the summit of Everest, built one in Khumjung). He learned the Nepali alphabet on his own and could barely write his own name.
 Ang Rita spent his childhood days in the high pastures grazing yaks, growing potatoes and carrying commodities from nearby markets. He became a porter when he was 15 and quickly gained a reputation for his agility, ultimately earning the nickname Snow Leopard.
 Although he was raised under the shadow of Mount Everest, his first job as a porter was to climb Dhaulagiri, a Himalayan massif that includes the world’s seventh-highest mountain, for which he had no shoes or equipment.
  In addition to climbing Everest 10 times, Ang Rita climbed Dhaulagiri a total of four times, as well as the Himalayan peak Cho Oyu — also four times — and Kanchenjunga, the third-highest peak, once. He didn’t use supplemental oxygen on any of these climbs.
 Ang Tshering, the former president of the Nepal Mountaineering Association, said in an interview that he once climbed Dhaulagiri with Ang Rita, and that he found him to be the strongest Sherpa of his time. “He challenged science and human physiology,” he said.
 Ang Rita stopped climbing after the 1996 Everest disaster, in which eight people died in a fierce blizzard. But he continued to work as a base camp manager and trekking guide.
 Colleagues say he never saved money or worried about the future. He lived a happy life and enjoyed his retirement days to the fullest. He was living in his home village until his wife, Nima Chokki, died several years ago. He then moved to Kathmandu to live with his daughter, Dolma.
 She survives him, as do two sons, Tshewang Dorje and Furunuru, and eight grandchildren. Another son, Karsang Namgyal Sherpa, who also became a mountain guide, died in 2012 after an Everest expedition.
title: 安格丽塔-1983-96年间在没有添加氧气的情况下10次攀登珠穆朗玛峰纪录-去世，享年72岁
title_en: Ang Rita–who climbed Everest record 10 times 1983-96 without added O2–dies at 72

汽车制造商正在花费数千万美元游说反对一项投票倡议，该倡议将确保独立维修公司在未来很长一段时间内保持修理汽车的能力。
这项名为问题1的措施将于11月进行投票，如果获得通过，独立修理店将能够无线访问汽车数据，以帮助促进汽车维修。令人担忧的是，汽车制造商将放弃用于诊断汽车问题的有线数据端口，这将阻止独立公司和车主评估汽车的问题所在。一个汽车制造商联盟已经在该州花费了超过2500万美元来挫败这项措施。
这一大规模行动背后的游说团体是安全和安全数据联盟(Coalition For Safe And Secure Data)，这是汽车创新联盟(Automotive Alliance For Innovation)的一个分支，由通用汽车(General Motors)、福特(Ford)、丰田(Toyota)、日产(Nissan)、本田(Honda)、现代(Hyundai)和斯巴鲁(Subaru。很多钱将用于播放可怕的广告，这些广告暗示如果问题1通过，罪犯可能会访问你的数据。
但问题1只是2012年的一项法律的更新，该法律允许独立维修商店通过汽车的车载诊断端口访问汽车的数据。这项立法成为国家标准，允许机械师合法维修车辆，即使它们不为制造商工作。问题1只是允许通过在现代汽车中变得越来越普遍的无线工具对相同的人进行同样的访问。
支持通过第一个问题的游说团体马萨诸塞州维修权委员会(Massachusetts Right To Repair Committee)也在该州筹集了大量资金。支持者已经从AutoZone和Advance Auto Parts等公司筹集了超过1500万美元。
SecuRepair是一个倡导安全和维修问题的安全和维修专业团体，在其网站上最近的一篇帖子中表示：“不用说，来自独立维修店的汽车维修服务竞争使服务和维修成本保持在较低水平。”SecuRepair是一个倡导安全和维修问题的安全和维修专业团体。“通过有线连接从车辆到维修店的计算机共享的相同机械数据也应该可以无线访问，这也是非常有意义的。”这就是为什么汽车制造商急于改变话题的原因。“。
马萨诸塞州维修权委员会(Massachusetts Right To Repair Committee)和SecuRepair等组织担心，问题1在11月的投票中失败将有助于汽车制造商建立并维持在维修领域的垄断地位。如果机械师不能访问你车里的数据，他们可能无法进行维修，而你将被困在经销商那里，那里可以收取他们想要的任何费用。
根据SecuRepair的说法，汽车业的广告对汽车制造商保护数据安全的能力提出了质疑，无论哪个机械师可以访问这些数据。SecuRepair在其网站上表示：“汽车制造商不应该试图吓唬消费者，而是应该让车主很容易获得这些数据，同时也应该透明地说明他们从智能汽车收集了什么数据，以及他们是如何使用这些数据的。”“事实和透明度，而不是恐惧，才是缓解公众对数据隐私和安全的焦虑的解毒剂。”
note_en:
Auto manufacturers are spending tens of millions of dollars lobbying against a ballot initiative that would ensure independent repair companies would retain the ability to fix cars well into the future.
  The measure, called Question 1, will be on the ballot in November and if it passes, independent repair stores will be able to access car data wirelessly to help facilitate vehicle repairs. The fear is that car manufacturers will move away from the wired data port used to diagnose car problems, which would prevent independent companies and car owners from assessing what's wrong with a car. A coalition of auto manufacturers have spent more than  $25 million in the state to defeat the measure.
   The lobbying group behind the big push is the Coalition for Safe and Secure Data, an arm of the  Automotive Alliance for Innovation which is funded by car manufacturers like General Motors, Ford, Toyota, Nissan, Honda, Hyundai, and Subaru. A lot of the money is going to  airing terrifying advertisements that imply criminals could access your data should Question 1 pass.
  But Question 1 is just an update to a 2012 law that allowed independent repair stores access to a car's data through the on-board diagnostics port in a car. That legislation became the national standard and allows mechanics to legally repair vehicles even if they don’t work for the manufacturer. Question 1 would simply allow the same access to the same people via the wireless tools that are becoming increasingly common in modern cars.
  The Massachusetts Right to Repair Committee, a lobbying group that supports the passage of Question 1, has also raised a lot of money in the state. Supporters have raised more than $15 million from companies like AutoZone and Advance Auto Parts.
  “It goes without saying that competition for vehicle repair and maintenance services from independent repair shops keeps the cost of service and repair down,” SecuRepairs—a group of security and repair professionals who advocate for security and repair issues— said in a recent post on its website. “It also makes perfect sense that the same mechanical data shared via a wired connection from a vehicle to a computer in a repair shop should also be accessible wirelessly. That’s why automakers are anxious to change the subject.”
  Groups such as the Massachusetts Right to Repair Committee and SecuRepairs fear that the failure of Question 1 at the ballot in November will help automakers build and maintain a monopoly on repair. If a mechanic can’t access the data in your car, they may not be able to make a repair and you’ll be stuck taking it into the dealership which can charge whatever they want.
  According to SecuRepairs, the auto industry’s ads raise questions about automakers' ability to keep data secure, regardless of which mechanic can access it. “Rather than trying to frighten consumers, car makers should make owner access to this data easy, while also being transparent about what data they are collecting from smart vehicles and how they use it,” SecuRepairs said on its website. “Facts and transparency, not fear, are the antidote for the public’s anxiety about data privacy and security.”
title: 汽车业花费2500万美元游说反对修复投票措施的权利
title_en: Auto Industry Has Spent $25M Lobbying Against Right to Repair Ballot Measure

note:
物理学家阿瑟·阿什金(Arthur Ashkin)于9月9日去世，他因发现如何利用光的力量捕捉微观物体进行更近距离的研究而获得2018年诺贝尔奖，他将自己的发明称为光镊子。21岁时，他在新泽西州拉姆森的家中，享年98岁。
光镊子-或者更确切地说是光学陷阱-利用高度聚焦的激光束的压力来操纵微观物体，从原子到活的有机体，比如病毒和细菌。
正如诺贝尔委员会所写的那样，阿什金博士“发明了用激光束手指抓取粒子、原子、分子和活细胞的光学镊子”。
诱捕生物材料被证明在研究和理解生命的基本构件，如DNA和其他生物系统的行为方面具有开创性的实际应用。今天，光学镊子被广泛制造并出售给研究人员。
阿什金博士的“镊子”是由一束激光--一束相干的单色光--通过一个微小的放大镜产生的。透镜为激光创造了一个焦点，由于大自然的一种奇怪的扭曲，粒子被吸引到这个焦点附近并被困在那里，不能上下或后退或向前移动。
斯坦福大学(Stanford University)生物学和应用物理学教授史蒂文·M·布洛克(Steven M.Block)将光镊子与“星际迷航”(Star Trek)和“星球大战”(Star Wars)中假设的那种固定技术相提并论，称它们是“人类制造出的最接近拖拉机光束的东西”。
1966年，当他去凤凰城参加一个科学会议时，他是贝尔实验室(Bell Labs)激光研究部门的负责人，贝尔实验室是1925年由贝尔电话公司(Bell Telephone Company)创立的传奇新泽西州实验室。在那里的一次讲座中，他听到两名研究人员在研究六年前发明的激光时发现了一些奇怪的东西：他们注意到激光束中的尘埃颗粒来回摆动。他们推论说，轻微的压力可能是起因。
阿什金博士做了一些计算，得出结论，这不是原因--很可能是热辐射。但他的作品重新燃起了童年时对轻压力这一主题的兴趣。
光推动一切事物，包括人，因为它由称为光子的微小粒子组成。大多数时候，压力是微不足道的；例如，人们感觉不到任何东西。但阿什金博士认为，如果物体足够小，可以用激光把它们推来推去。
他用一个微小的透明玻璃球进行实验，激光的光子可以通过它，并发现他确实能够推动它。但出乎意料的是，球体被吸引到光束的中心，在那里它被困住了。
这其中的原因与物理学中一个不变的定律有关：动量守恒。当光子穿过球体并被其偏转时，球体向与偏转的光子相反的方向移动。由于光束中心有更多光子，球体被推向中心。
阿什金博士意识到，通过使用两束相互对准的光束，就有可能捕获微小物体并移动它们。1970年，一篇关于他的发现的里程碑式的文章发表在“物理评论快报”上。
阿什金博士和他在贝尔实验室的同事们继续研究这个课题，1980年，他想出了一种使用光学陷阱来测量电子电荷的方法。
然后，在1986年，他和几位同事，特别是朱，实现了光学镊子的第一次实际应用，他们通过透镜发送激光来操纵微观物体。他们的研究结果发表在“物理评论快报”的另一篇论文上。朱博士开始使用镊子来冷却和捕获原子，这一突破使他在1997年获得了诺贝尔物理学奖的三分之一。
很明显，阿什金博士对诺贝尔委员会没有认可他在颁奖过程中的基础性工作感到恼火。但他已经开始将镊子用于不同的目的：捕捉活的有机体和生物材料。
其他科学家认为这一应用不会奏效，正如他在2018年获奖后接受诺贝尔研究所采访时所解释的那样。
“他们用光来治愈伤口，这被认为是致命的，”他说。“当我描述用光捕捉生物时，人们说，‘不要夸张，阿什金。’”
阿什金博士能够捕捉到的东西包括单细胞草履虫的亚细胞结构和一种攻击烟草植物的小型病毒。通过他的捕获方法，也可以观察到DNA的复制。
阿什金博士获得了2018年物理学奖的一半，与法国的热拉尔·穆鲁(Gérard Mourou)和加拿大的唐娜·斯特里克兰(Donna Strickland)分享，这两人分别获得了四分之一的奖金。在这样做的过程中，96岁的他成为当时年龄最大的诺贝尔奖获得者。(次年，97岁的约翰·B·古德足够获得诺贝尔化学奖。)。
由于无法出席在斯德哥尔摩举行的诺贝尔奖颁奖典礼，阿什金博士在新泽西州的诺基亚贝尔实验室发表了他的诺贝尔演讲。
亚瑟·阿什金出生于9月1日。1922年2月2日，在布鲁克林，伊萨多·阿什金和安娜·阿什金的四个孩子中的一个，他们有乌克兰犹太血统。他的哥哥朱利叶斯也成为了一名物理学家，并在曼哈顿计划中发挥了重要作用，曼哈顿计划是第二次世界大战期间研制原子弹的秘密努力。
从詹姆斯·麦迪逊高中毕业后，亚瑟跟随朱利叶斯来到哥伦比亚大学。他在哥伦比亚辐射实验室工作，研究磁控管，磁控管产生微波，是激光的先驱。当时，另外两名未来的诺贝尔奖获得者正在实验室工作。
阿什金博士于1947年从哥伦比亚大学毕业，在康奈尔大学学习核物理，在那里他与汉斯·贝斯(Hans Bethe)和理查德·费曼(Richard Feynman)共事，这两人都是未来的诺贝尔奖获得者。他于1952年从康奈尔大学获得博士学位后加入贝尔实验室，并在那里工作到1992年退休。他从1963年到1987年领导实验室的激光科学部。
除了光学镊子，阿什金博士还被誉为发现了光折变效应，这种效应暂时改变了材料，特别是晶体，散射或弯曲光线的方式。这种效果的实际用途包括创建临时全息图。科学家们认为，这可能会导致功能更强大的计算机，使用光而不是电来存储数据。
阿什金博士拥有47项专利，并于2013年入选国家发明家名人堂(National Inventors Hall Of Fame)。
除了女儿朱迪思，他在世的亲人还有妻子阿琳·阿什金(Aline Ashkin)，她曾是一名高中化学教授，教过贝尔实验室员工的许多孩子；他们的儿子丹尼尔(Daniel)和迈克尔(Michael)是一名艺术家，他的作品曾在纽约惠特尼美国艺术博物馆(Whitney Museum Of American Art)的双年展中展出；五个孙辈；以及两个曾孙辈。
阿什金博士从贝尔实验室退休并没有阻止他继续他的研究。当他收到诺贝尔奖的消息时，他正在他的地下室里做一个改善太阳能收集的项目。当被问及是否会庆祝时，他说：“我现在正在写论文。我不是在庆祝古老的东西。“
note_en:
Arthur Ashkin, a physicist who was awarded  a 2018 Nobel Prize for figuring out how to harness the power of light to trap microscopic objects for closer study, calling his invention optical tweezers, died on Sept. 21 at his home in Rumson, N.J. He was 98.
  Optical tweezers — or optical traps, as they are more properly known — use the pressure from a highly focused laser beam to manipulate microscopic objects, from atoms to living organisms, like viruses and bacteria.
 As the Nobel committee wrote, Dr. Ashkin had “invented optical tweezers that grab particles, atoms, molecules, and living cells with their laser beam fingers.”
 Trapping biological material proved to have groundbreaking practical applications in research and in understanding the behavior of the basic building blocks of life, like DNA, and other biological systems. Today, optical tweezers are widely manufactured and sold to researchers.
 Dr. Ashkin’s “tweezer” is created by shining a laser — a beam of coherent monochromatic light — through a tiny magnifying lens. The lens creates a focal point for the laser, and, by a strange twist of nature, particles are drawn near that focal point and trapped there, unable to move up or down or backward or forward.
 Steven M. Block, a professor of biology and applied physics at Stanford University, compared optical tweezers to the kind of immobilizing technology postulated in “Star Trek” and “Star Wars,” calling them “the closest thing to a tractor beam that humans have ever produced.”
  In 1966, he was head of the laser research department at Bell Labs, the storied New Jersey laboratory founded by the Bell Telephone Company in 1925, when he went to a scientific conference in Phoenix. There, in a lecture, he heard two researchers  discuss something odd that they had found while studying lasers, which had been invented six years earlier: They had noticed that dust particles within the laser beams careened back and forth. They theorized that light pressure might be the cause.
 Dr. Ashkin did some calculations and concluded that this was not the cause — it was most likely thermal radiation. But his work reignited a childhood interest in the subject of light pressure.
 Light pushes against everything, including people, because it comprises tiny particles called photons. Most of the time the pressure is utterly insignificant; people, for one, feel nothing. But Dr. Ashkin thought that if objects were small enough, a laser might be used to push them around.
  He experimented with a tiny transparent glass sphere through which the photons of the laser could pass, and found that he was indeed able to push it around. But unexpectedly the sphere gravitated toward the center of the beam, where it became trapped.
 The reason had to do with one of the immutable laws of physics: the conservation of momentum. As the photons passed through the sphere and were deflected by it, the sphere moved in the opposite direction of the deflected photons. Since there were more photons at the center of the beam, the sphere was driven toward the center.
 Dr. Ashkin realized that by using two beams pointed at each other, it would be possible to trap tiny objects and move them around. A  landmark article about his discovery was published in Physical Review Letters in 1970.
  Dr. Ashkin continued to research the subject along with his colleagues at Bell Labs, and in 1980 he came up with a way to use optical traps to measure the charge of an electron.
 Then, in 1986, he and several colleagues, notably  Steven Chu, achieved the first practical application of optical tweezers when they sent a laser through a lens to manipulate microscopic objects. Their results were published  in another paper in Physical Review Letters. Dr. Chu began using the tweezers to  cool and trap atoms, a breakthrough for which he was awarded a one-third share of the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1997.
 Dr. Ashkin, it was clear, was irked that the Nobel committee had not recognized his foundational work in awarding the prize. But he had already begun to use the tweezers for a different purpose: trapping live organisms and biological material.
 Other scientists thought this application would not work, as he explained in an interview with the Nobel Institute after he was awarded the prize in 2018.
 “They used light to heal wounds, and it was considered to be deadly,” he said. “When I described catching living things with light, people said, ‘Don’t exaggerate, Ashkin.’”
 Among the things Dr. Ashkin was able to trap were subcellular structures of the single-celled paramecium and a small virus that attacks tobacco plants. Through his trapping method it also became possible to observe DNA being replicated.
 Dr. Ashkin was awarded one-half the 2018 physics prize, sharing it with Gérard Mourou of France and Donna Strickland of Canada, who each received a quarter of it. In so doing he became, at 96, the oldest recipient of a Nobel Prize at the time. (The next year,  John B. Goodenough received the Nobel in chemistry at 97.)
 Unable to attend the Nobel ceremony in Stockholm,  Dr. Ashkin delivered his Nobel lecture from the Nokia Bell Laboratories in New Jersey.
 Arthur Ashkin was born on Sept. 2, 1922, in Brooklyn, one of four children of Isadore and Anna Ashkin, who were of Ukrainian-Jewish heritage. His older brother, Julius,  also became a physicist and played an important role in the Manhattan Project, the secret effort during World War II to develop the atomic bomb.
 After graduating from James Madison High School, Arthur followed Julius to Columbia University. He worked in the Columbia Radiation Laboratory on magnetrons, which produced microwaves and were a precursor to the laser. Two other future Nobel laureates were working in the lab at the time.
 Dr. Ashkin graduated from Columbia in 1947 and studied nuclear physics at Cornell, where he worked with  Hans Bethe and Richard Feynman, both future Nobel laureates as well. He joined Bell Labs after obtaining his Ph.D. from Cornell in 1952 and worked there until his retirement in 1992. He led the lab’s laser science department from 1963 to 1987.
 In addition to optical tweezers, Dr. Ashkin was credited with discovering the  photorefractive effect, which temporarily alters how materials, notably crystals, scatter or bend light. The practical uses of the effect include creating temporary holograms. Scientists believe that it could lead to more powerful computers that would use light, rather than electricity, to store data.
 Dr. Ashkin held 47 patents and was inducted into the National Inventors Hall of Fame in 2013.
 In addition to his daughter, Judith, he is survived by his wife, Aline Ashkin, a former high school chemistry professor who taught many of the children of Bell Labs employees; their sons, Daniel and Michael, an artist whose work has appeared in biennials at the Whitney Museum of American Art in New York; five grandchildren; and two great-grandchildren.
 Dr. Ashkin’s retirement from Bell Labs did not stop him from continuing his research. When he received word of his Nobel Prize, he was working on a project in his basement to improve solar energy collection. Asked if he was going to celebrate, he said: “I am writing a paper right now. I am not about celebrating old stuff.”
title: A·阿什金(A.Ashkin)去世，享年98岁。阿什金于1970年发明“拖拉机光束”，导致2018年诺贝尔奖
title_en: A. Ashkin – whose 1970 invention of 'tractor beam' led to 2018 Nobel –dies at 98

路透哥本哈根5月15日-丹麦周二表示，该国可以在不牺牲其慷慨福利的情况下，实现2030年减排70%的气候目标，这是全球最雄心勃勃的目标之一。
去年，两党通过了一项法律，承诺丹麦在10年内将温室气体排放量在1990年的基础上减少70%，即约2000万吨二氧化碳当量。
在周二发布的一份气候计划中，政府估计，到2030年，实施向绿色技术转变的年度成本将升至160亿至240亿丹麦克朗(约合25亿至37亿美元)，占国内生产总值(GDP)的0.7%至1.0%。
气候部长Dan Joergensen在一份声明中说：“我们雄心勃勃的气候目标并非没有成本，但只要有了明智的方法，账单就可以变得更小、更好地管理，这样我们就可以同时负担气候和福利。”
政府表示，去年启动的举措将减少约500万吨二氧化碳当量。
该公司表示，通过使用碳捕获储存和“电力到X”等新技术，可以再减少900万-1650万吨电力-将多余的电力转化为过剩的电力，通常来自可再生能源，如风能，丹麦有大量的电力，方法是利用多余的电力生产可储存的物质或燃料，如氢或甲烷。
政府表示，在企业中不受欢迎的碳排放税将于今年晚些时候谈判。
丹麦政府的独立顾问丹麦气候变化理事会建议大幅提高目前的碳税，以使丹麦实现其目标。

COPENHAGEN (Reuters) - Denmark said on Tuesday that it could reach its 2030 climate target of reducing emissions by 70%, one of the world’s most ambitious, without compromising its generous welfare benefits.
 Last year, parties across the aisle passed a law committing Denmark to reduce greenhouse gas emissions by 70% from 1990 levels, or around 20 million tonnes of CO2 equivalent, within 10 years.
 In a climate plan published on Tuesday, the government estimated that the annual cost of implementing the shift to greener technologies would rise to 16-24 billion Danish crowns ($2.5-$3.7 billion) by 2030 - or 0.7%-1.0% of gross domestic product.
  “Our ambitious climate goals are not without costs, but with a wise approach, the bill can be made smaller and managed so that we can afford both climate and welfare,” Climate Minister Dan Joergensen said in a statement.
 Initiatives launched in the last year will cut around 5 million tonnes of CO2 equivalent, the government said.
 It said another 9-16.5 million tonnes could be cut by using new technologies such as carbon capture storage and ‘power-to-X’ - converting surplus electricity, usually from renewable sources such as wind, plentiful in Denmark, by using it to produce storable substances or fuels such as hydrogen or methane.
  Levies on carbon emissions, unpopular among businesses, will be negotiated later this year, the government said.
 The Danish Council on Climate Change, an independent adviser to the government, recommends sharply increasing the current carbon tax to get Denmark to meet its target.
title: 丹麦：我们可以在十年内削减70%的二氧化碳，同时仍然有福利
title_en: Denmark: We can slash CO2 by 70% in a decade and still have welfare
